---
title: "Ultramarathon Runner Age vs. Performance"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "Contents"
    self-contained: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
jupyter: python3
---
## READ IN DATA
```{python}
import requests
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import seaborn as sns
from io import StringIO

RANKINGS_URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/"
    "master/data/2021/2021-10-26/ultra_rankings.csv")
RACE_URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/"
    "master/data/2021/2021-10-26/race.csv")
rankings = pd.read_csv(RANKINGS_URL)
races    = pd.read_csv(RACE_URL)

print(f"Rankings rows : {len(rankings):,}")
print(f"Race rows     : {len(races):,}")
rankings.head(3)
```

## CLEAN DATA
```{python}
# merge and filter
df = rankings.merge(
    races[["race_year_id", "distance", "date", "country"]],
    on="race_year_id",
    how="left",)
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df["year"] = df["date"].dt.year

# keep only 100 km+ races, sensible ages, valid finish times
df = df[
    (df["distance"] >= 100)
    & (df["age"].between(18, 80))
    & (df["time_in_seconds"] > 0)
    & (df["gender"].isin(["M", "W"]))
].copy()

# convert seconds to H:MM:SS
def seconds_to_hms(s):
    s = int(s)
    h, rem = divmod(s, 3600)
    m, sec = divmod(rem, 60)
    return f"{h}:{m:02d}:{sec:02d}"
df["finish_time"] = df["time_in_seconds"].apply(seconds_to_hms)

# convert seconds to numeric hours
df["hours"] = df["time_in_seconds"] / 3600

def fmt_name(name):
    if not isinstance(name, str) or " " not in name:
        return name
    parts = name.split()
    last  = parts[0].capitalize()
    first = " ".join(p.capitalize() for p in parts[1:])
    return f"{last} {first}"
df["runner"] = df["runner"].apply(fmt_name)

# age groups
bins   = [18, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 80]
labels = ["18–24", "25–29", "30–34", "35–39",
          "40–44", "45–49", "50–54", "55–59", "60–64", "65+"]
df["age_group"] = pd.cut(df["age"], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)

# elite tier (top 10% by finish time within each race × gender)
df["pace_pct"] = df.groupby(["race_year_id", "gender"])["time_in_seconds"].rank(pct=True)
df["is_elite"] = df["pace_pct"] <= 0.10

print(f"Filtered dataset: {len(df):,} performances")
print(f"  Men   : {(df.gender=='M').sum():,}")
print(f"  Women : {(df.gender=='W').sum():,}")
print(f"  Years : {df.year.min()} – {df.year.max()}")

df[["runner", "age", "gender", "finish_time", "age_group", "is_elite"]].tail()
```

## PLOT FINISH TIME BY AGE GROUP
```{python}
# figure 1: median finish time by age group (all finishers)
GENDER_COLORS = {"M": "#1a6faf", "W": "#c0392b"}
GENDER_LABELS = {"M": "Men", "W": "Women"}

med = (
    df.groupby(["age_group", "gender"], observed=True)["hours"]
    .median()
    .reset_index())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.5))
for g, grp in med.groupby("gender"):
    ax.plot(
        grp["age_group"].astype(str),
        grp["hours"],
        marker="o",
        linewidth=2.5,
        markersize=8,
        color=GENDER_COLORS[g],
        label=GENDER_LABELS[g],
    )
    # annotate the minimum (best) age group
    best_idx = grp["hours"].idxmin()
    best_row = grp.loc[best_idx]
    ax.annotate(
        f"Best: {best_row['hours']:.1f} h",
        xy=(str(best_row["age_group"]), best_row["hours"]),
        xytext=(0, -22),
        textcoords="offset points",
        ha="center",
        fontsize=9,
        color=GENDER_COLORS[g],
        arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color=GENDER_COLORS[g], lw=1),
    )

ax.set_title("Median Finish Time by Age Group\n(all 100 km+ finishers, 2012–2021)",
             fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Age Group", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Median Finish Time (hours)", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FormatStrFormatter("%.0f h"))
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
sns.despine()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Both men's and women's fastest times are recorded in the 18–24 bracket, and the times increase through the 30s and 40s before rising steeply in the 50s. However, because this is the overall population that mixes recreational runners and elites, the 18–24 group is tiny and extremely self-selected (very few young runners enter 100km+ races). The median times remain steady through the 25–44 brackets, which are the most popular and competitive age groups. The apparent performance decline after 45 may be exaggerated because there are fewer elite runners at older ages and the field becomes more recreational.

## PLOT ELITE VS. ALL FINISHERS
```{python}
# figure 2: elite vs. all finishers comparison
all_med = (
    df.groupby(["age_group", "gender"], observed=True)["hours"]
    .median()
    .reset_index()
    .assign(tier="All finishers")
)
elite_med = (
    df[df["is_elite"]]
    .groupby(["age_group", "gender"], observed=True)["hours"]
    .median()
    .reset_index()
    .assign(tier="Elite (top 10 %)")
)
combined = pd.concat([all_med, elite_med])
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5.5), sharey=False)

for ax, gender, title in zip(axes, ["M", "W"], ["Men", "Women"]):
    sub = combined[combined["gender"] == gender]
    for tier, grp in sub.groupby("tier"):
        style = "-o" if tier == "Elite (top 10 %)" else "--s"
        lw    = 2.5 if tier == "Elite (top 10 %)" else 1.5
        alpha = 1.0 if tier == "Elite (top 10 %)" else 0.55
        ax.plot(
            grp["age_group"].astype(str),
            grp["hours"],
            style,
            linewidth=lw,
            markersize=7,
            color=GENDER_COLORS[gender],
            alpha=alpha,
            label=tier,
        )
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("Age Group", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel("Median Finish Time (hours)", fontsize=11)
    ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=35)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    sns.despine(ax=ax)
fig.suptitle("Elite vs. All Finisher Performance Curves", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Among elite finishers, the pattern is more aligned with the hypothesis. Performance peaks in the 25–34 age window and declines gradually. Young runners in the elite tier are not always faster than their counterparts in their 30s and early 40s.

## PLOT ELITE AGE DISTRIBUTION
```{python}
# figure 3: age distribution of top-10% finishers
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

for ax, (gender, label) in zip(axes, [("M", "Men"), ("W", "Women")]):
    ages = df[(df["is_elite"]) & (df["gender"] == gender)]["age"]
    ax.hist(ages, bins=range(18, 75, 2), color=GENDER_COLORS[gender],
            edgecolor="white", alpha=0.85)
    ax.axvline(ages.median(), color="black", lw=2, linestyle="--",
               label=f"Median: {ages.median():.0f} yrs")
    ax.axvline(ages.mean(), color="grey", lw=1.5, linestyle=":",
               label=f"Mean:  {ages.mean():.1f} yrs")
    ax.set_title(f"Elite {label} — Age Distribution", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("Age", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel("Number of Finishes", fontsize=11)
    ax.legend(fontsize=10)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    sns.despine(ax=ax)

plt.suptitle("Age of the Fastest Ultramarathon Runners", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

elite_ages = df[df["is_elite"]].groupby("gender")["age"].describe()
print(elite_ages[["mean","50%","25%","75%"]].rename(columns={"50%":"median"}))
```

The median age of an elite ultramarathon finisher is 42 years for both men and women. How unique is it that a runner is still competitive at an age when most professional athletes have been retired for years?

## PLOT PARTICIPATION NUMBERS BY AGE GROUP
```{python}
# figure 4: raw finisher count by age group
yearly_counts = (
    df.groupby(["year", "age_group"], observed=True)
    .size()
    .reset_index(name="n"))

wide = yearly_counts.pivot(index="year", columns="age_group", values="n").fillna(0)
wide = wide[[l for l in labels if l in wide.columns]]

cmap   = plt.get_cmap("tab10")
colors = cmap(np.linspace(0, 0.9, len(labels)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.5))

ax.stackplot(wide.index, wide.T, labels=wide.columns, colors=colors, alpha=0.85)
ax.set_title("Finisher Count by Age Group (All Runners)", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Finishers", fontsize=11)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{int(x):,}"))
ax.legend(title="Age Group", fontsize=9, ncols=2, loc="upper left")
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
ax.set_xticks(range(2012, 2022))
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=40)
sns.despine()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The 40–44 age range has the highest finisher count among all runners.

## VIEW SUMMARY STATISTICS
```{python}
summary = (
    df.groupby(["age_group", "gender"], observed=True)
    .agg(
        n_finishes    = ("hours", "count"),
        median_hours  = ("hours", "median"),
        mean_hours    = ("hours", "mean"),
        pct_elite     = ("is_elite", "mean"),)
    .reset_index())
summary["pct_elite"] = (summary["pct_elite"] * 100).round(1)
summary["median_hours"] = summary["median_hours"].round(2)
summary["mean_hours"]   = summary["mean_hours"].round(2)

for g, label in [("M","Men"), ("W","Women")]:
    print(f"\n{'─'*55}")
    print(f"  {label}")
    print(f"{'─'*55}")
    print(
        summary[summary["gender"] == g]
        [["age_group","n_finishes","median_hours","mean_hours","pct_elite"]]
        .to_string(index=False))
```

This analysis of 111,146 finish records from 100km+ ultramarathons supports the claim that elite ultramarathon performance does not follow the early-twenties peak model of most sports. Instead, the data reveals a distinct age-performance curve with several key insights:
1. Though young runners post faster median times when compared to all racers, they constitute a much smaller proportion of finishers than later age brackets, potentially skewing the data in their favor. Only gifted young runners are likely to complete in 100km+ races.
2. Elite performers cluster around age 42. Ultramarathon elites are older than the general elite in almost every other major sport. This aligns with the literature on slow-twitch fiber development, fat oxidation efficiency, and pain tolerance, which typically improve well into the 40s.
3. Older runners are a growing share of the field. The 40–59 age groups increased their representation from 2012 to 2021, among both men and women.
4. Performance decline is gradual and late. Meaningful performance decline (measured by mean hours) begins after 44 for men and after 49 for women, much later than in sprint or power sports.

## REFERENCES
"Does Age Matter in Ultramarathon?" *Runners Connect*, runnersconnect.net/does-age-matter-in-ultramarathon/. Accessed 13 June 2026.
Knechtle, Beat, et al. "Age-Related Changes in 100-km Ultra-Marathon Running Performance." *Age*, vol. 35, no. 3, 2013, pp. 1063–1083, pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3682063/.
McDougall, Christopher. *Born to Run: A Hidden Tribe, Superathletes, and the Greatest Race the World Has Never Seen*. Knopf, 2009.
"Ultra Trail Running Dataset." *TidyTuesday*, 26 Oct. 2021, github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/tree/master/data/2021/2021-10-26.
"Why Do Ultra-Endurance Athletes Get Better with Age?" *Born on the Trail*, bornonthetrail.substack.com/p/why-do-ultra-endurance-athletes-get-better-with-age. Accessed 13 June 2026.
